text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.

3	description: Learn how to cache and revalidate data in your application.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/fetch

14	---
15	
16	Caching is a technique for storing the result of data fetching and other computations so that future requests for the same data can be served faster, without doing the work again. While revalidation allows you to update cache entries without having to rebuild your entire application.
17	
18	Next.js provides a few APIs to handle caching and revalidation. This guide will walk you through when and how to use them.
19	
20	- [`fetch`](#fetch)
21	- [`cacheTag`](#cachetag)
22	- [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag)
23	- [`updateTag`](#updatetag)
24	- [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)
25	- [`unstable_cache`](#unstable_cache) (Legacy)
26	
27	## `fetch`
28	
29	By default, [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) requests are not cached. You can cache individual requests by setting the `cache` option to `'force-cache'`.
30	
31	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
32	export default async function Page() {
33	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
34	}
35	```
36	
37	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
38	export default async function Page() {
39	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
40	}
41	```
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Although `fetch` requests are not cached by default, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes that have `fetch` requests and cache the HTML. If you want to guarantee a route is [dynamic](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the [`connection` API](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection).
44	
45	To revalidate the data returned by a `fetch` request, you can use the `next.revalidate` option.
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
48	export default async function Page() {
49	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
50	}
51	```
52	
53	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
54	export default async function Page() {
55	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
56	}
57	```
58	
59	This will revalidate the data after a specified amount of seconds.
60	
61	You can also tag `fetch` requests to enable on-demand cache invalidation:
62	
63	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
64	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
65	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
66	    next: {
67	      tags: ['user'],
68	    },
69	  })
70	}
71	```
72	
73	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
74	export async function getUserById(id) {
75	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
76	    next: {
77	      tags: ['user'],
78	    },
79	  })
80	}
81	```
82	
83	See the [`fetch` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) to learn more.
84	
85	## `cacheTag`
86	
87	[`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) allows you to tag cached data in [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) so it can be revalidated on-demand. Previously, cache tagging was limited to `fetch` requests, and caching other work required the experimental `unstable_cache` API.
88	
89	With Cache Components, you can use the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to cache any computation, and `cacheTag` to tag it. This works with database queries, file system operations, and other server-side work.
90	
91	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
92	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
93	
94	export async function getProducts() {
95	  'use cache'
96	  cacheTag('products')
97	
98	  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products')
99	  return products
100	}
101	```
102	
103	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
104	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
105	
106	export async function getProducts() {
107	  'use cache'
108	  cacheTag('products')
109	
110	  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products')
111	  return products
112	}
113	```
114	
115	Once tagged, you can use [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag) or [`updateTag`](#updatetag) to invalidate the cache entry for products.
116	
117	> **Good to know**: `cacheTag` is used with [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) and the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive. It expands the caching and revalidation story beyond `fetch`.
118	
119	See the [`cacheTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) to learn more.
120	
121	## `revalidateTag`
122	
123	`revalidateTag` is used to revalidate cache entries based on a tag and following an event. The function now supports two behaviors:
124	
125	- **With `profile="max"`**: Uses stale-while-revalidate semantics, serving stale content while fetching fresh content in the background
126	- **Without the second argument**: Legacy behavior that immediately expires the cache (deprecated)
127	
128	After tagging your cached data, using [`fetch`](#fetch) with `next.tags`, or the [`cacheTag`](#cachetag) function, you may call `revalidateTag` in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or Server Action:
129	
130	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1,5} switcher
131	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
132	
133	export async function updateUser(id: string) {
134	  // Mutate data
135	  revalidateTag('user', 'max') // Recommended: Uses stale-while-revalidate
136	}
137	```
138	
139	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1,5} switcher
140	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
141	
142	export async function updateUser(id) {
143	  // Mutate data
144	  revalidateTag('user', 'max') // Recommended: Uses stale-while-revalidate
145	}
146	```
147	
148	You can reuse the same tag in multiple functions to revalidate them all at once.
149	
150	See the [`revalidateTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) to learn more.
151	
152	## `updateTag`
153	
154	`updateTag` is specifically designed for Server Actions to immediately expire cached data for read-your-own-writes scenarios. Unlike `revalidateTag`, it can only be used within Server Actions and immediately expires the cache entry.
155	
156	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1,6} switcher
157	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
158	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
159	
160	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
161	  // Create post in database
162	  const post = await db.post.create({
163	    data: {
164	      title: formData.get('title'),
165	      content: formData.get('content'),
166	    },
167	  })
168	
169	  // Immediately expire cache so the new post is visible
170	  updateTag('posts')
171	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
172	
173	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
174	}
175	```
176	
177	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1,6} switcher
178	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
179	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
180	
181	export async function createPost(formData) {
182	  // Create post in database
183	  const post = await db.post.create({
184	    data: {
185	      title: formData.get('title'),
186	      content: formData.get('content'),
187	    },
188	  })
189	
190	  // Immediately expire cache so the new post is visible
191	  updateTag('posts')
192	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
193	
194	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
195	}
196	```
197	
198	The key differences between `revalidateTag` and `updateTag`:
199	
200	- **`updateTag`**: Only in Server Actions, immediately expires cache, for read-your-own-writes
201	- **`revalidateTag`**: In Server Actions and Route Handlers, supports stale-while-revalidate with `profile="max"`
202	
203	See the [`updateTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) to learn more.
204	
205	## `revalidatePath`
206	
207	`revalidatePath` is used to revalidate a route and following an event. To use it, call it in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or Server Action:
208	
209	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1} switcher
210	import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'
211	
212	export async function updateUser(id: string) {
213	  // Mutate data
214	  revalidatePath('/profile')
215	```
216	
217	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1} switcher
218	import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'
219	
220	export async function updateUser(id) {
221	  // Mutate data
222	  revalidatePath('/profile')
223	```
224	
225	See the [`revalidatePath` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) to learn more.
226	
227	## `unstable_cache`
228	
229	> **Good to know**: `unstable_cache` is an experimental API. We recommend opting into [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) and replacing `unstable_cache` with the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive. See the [Cache Components documentation](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) for more details.
230	
231	`unstable_cache` allows you to cache the result of database queries and other async functions. To use it, wrap `unstable_cache` around the function. For example:
232	
233	```ts filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
234	import { db } from '@/lib/db'
235	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
236	  return db
237	    .select()
238	    .from(users)
239	    .where(eq(users.id, id))
240	    .then((res) => res[0])
241	}
242	```
243	
244	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
245	import { db } from '@/lib/db'
246	
247	export async function getUserById(id) {
248	  return db
249	    .select()
250	    .from(users)
251	    .where(eq(users.id, id))
252	    .then((res) => res[0])
253	}
254	```
255	
256	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={2,11,13} switcher
257	import { unstable_cache } from 'next/cache'
258	import { getUserById } from '@/app/lib/data'
259	
260	export default async function Page({
261	  params,
262	}: {
263	  params: Promise<{ userId: string }>
264	}) {
265	  const { userId } = await params
266	
267	  const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
268	    async () => {
269	      return getUserById(userId)
270	    },
271	    [userId] // add the user ID to the cache key
272	  )
273	}
274	```
275	
276	```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={2,7,9} switcher
277	import { unstable_cache } from 'next/cache'
278	import { getUserById } from '@/app/lib/data'
279	
280	export default async function Page({ params }) {
281	  const { userId } = await params
282	
283	  const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
284	    async () => {
285	      return getUserById(userId)
286	    },
287	    [userId] // add the user ID to the cache key
288	  )
289	}
290	```
291	
292	The function accepts a third optional object to define how the cache should be revalidated. It accepts:
293	
294	- `tags`: an array of tags used by Next.js to revalidate the cache.
295	- `revalidate`: the number of seconds after cache should be revalidated.
296	
297	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={6-9} switcher
298	const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
299	  async () => {
300	    return getUserById(userId)
301	  },
302	  [userId],
303	  {
304	    tags: ['user'],
305	    revalidate: 3600,
306	  }
307	)
308	```
309	
310	```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={6-9} switcher
311	const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
312	  async () => {
313	    return getUserById(userId)
314	  },
315	  [userId],
316	  {
317	    tags: ['user'],
318	    revalidate: 3600,
319	  }
320	)
321	```
322	
323	See the [`unstable_cache` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache) to learn more.

Moving this work to the client trades server load for larger bundles and slower initial rendering. 21 22 Cache Components eliminates these tradeoffs by prerendering routes into a **static HTML shell** that's immediately sent to the browser, with dynamic content updating the UI as it becomes ready. 23 24 31 32 ## How rendering works with Cache Components 33 34 At build time, Next.js renders your route's component tree. As long as components don't access network resources, certain system APIs, or require an incoming request to render, their output is **automatically added to the static shell**. Otherwise, you must choose how to handle them: 35 36 - Either defer rendering to request time by wrapping components in React's [``](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense), [showing fallback UI](#defer-rendering-to-request-time) until the content is ready 37 - Or cache the result using the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to [include it in the static shell](#using-use-cache) (if no request data is needed) 38 39 Because this happens ahead of time, before a request arrives, we refer to it as prerendering. This generates a static shell consisting of HTML for initial page loads and a serialized [RSC Payload](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#on-the-server) for client-side navigation, ensuring the browser receives fully rendered content instantly whether users navigate directly to the URL or transition from another page. 40 41 Next.js requires you to explicitly handle components that can't complete during prerendering. This is ideal for static content that can tolerate eventual consistency. 412 413 ```tsx filename="app/actions.ts" highlight={1,4,5,12} 414 import { cacheTag, revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 415 416 export async function getPosts() { 417 'use cache' 418 cacheTag('posts') 419 // fetch data 420 } 421 422 export async function createPost(post: FormData) { 423 'use server' 424 // write data using the FormData 425 revalidateTag('posts', 'max') 426 } 427 ``` 428 429 For more detailed explanation and usage examples, see the [`use cache` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache). 430 431 ### What should I cache? 432 433 What you cache should be a function of what you want your UI loading states to be. If data doesn't depend on runtime data and you're okay with a cached value being served for multiple requests over a period of time, use `use cache` with `cacheLife` to describe that behavior. 434 435 For content management systems with update mechanisms, consider using tags with longer cache durations and rely on `revalidateTag` to mark static initial UI as ready for revalidation. This pattern allows you to serve fast, cached responses while still updating content when it actually changes, rather than expiring the cache preemptively. 436 437 ## Putting it all together 438 439 Here's a complete example showing static content, cached dynamic content, and streaming dynamic content working together on a single page: 440 441 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" 442 import { Suspense } from 'react' 443 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 444 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 445 import Link from 'next/link' 446 447 export default function BlogPage() { 448 return ( 449 <> 450 {/* Static content - prerendered automatically */} 451

Only the personalized preferences need to stream in at request time since they depend on the user's cookies. This ensures fast initial page loads while still providing personalized content. 511 512 ## Metadata and Viewport 513 514 `generateMetadata` and `generateViewport` are part of rendering your page or layout. During prerendering, their access to runtime data or uncached dynamic data is tracked separately from the rest of the page. 515 516 If a page or layout is prerenderable but only metadata or viewport accesses uncached dynamic data or runtime data, Next.js requires an explicit choice: cache the data if possible, or signal that deferred rendering is intentional. See [Metadata with Cache Components](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#with-cache-components) and [Viewport with Cache Components](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport#with-cache-components) for how to handle this. 517 518 ## Enabling Cache Components 519 520 You can enable Cache Components (which includes PPR) by adding the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) option to your Next config file: 521 522 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" highlight={4} switcher 523 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 524 525 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 526 cacheComponents: true, 527 } 528 529 export default nextConfig 530 ``` 531 532 ```js filename="next.config.js" highlight={3} switcher 533 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 534 const nextConfig = { 535 cacheComponents: true, 536 } 537 538 module.exports = nextConfig 539 ``` 540 541 > **Good to know:** When Cache Components is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same prerendering model as pages. See [Route Handlers with Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/route-handlers#with-cache-components) for details. 542 543 ## Navigation uses Activity 544 545 When the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) flag is enabled, Next.js uses React's [``](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity) component to preserve component state during client-side navigation. 546 547 Rather than unmounting the previous route when you navigate away, Next.js sets the Activity mode to [`"hidden"`](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity#activity). This means: 548 549 - Component state is preserved when navigating between routes 550 - When you navigate back, the previous route reappears with its state intact 551 - Effects are cleaned up when a route is hidden, and recreated when it becomes visible again 552 553 This behavior improves the navigation experience by maintaining UI state (form inputs, or expanded sections) when users navigate back and forth between routes. 554 555 > **Good to know**: Next.js uses heuristics to keep a few recently visited routes `"hidden"`, while older routes are removed from the DOM to prevent excessive growth. 556 557 ## Migrating route segment configs 558 559 When Cache Components is enabled, several route segment config options are no longer needed or supported: 560 561 ### `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` 562 563 **Not needed.** All pages are dynamic by default. 564 565 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 566 // Before - No longer needed 567 export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' 568 569 export default function Page() { 570 return
...
571 } 572 ``` 573 574 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 575 // After - Just remove it 576 export default function Page() { 577 return
...
578 } 579 ``` 580 581 ### `dynamic = "force-static"` 582 583 Start by removing it. When unhandled dynamic or runtime data access is detected during development and build time, Next.js raises an error. Otherwise, the [prerendering](#automatically-prerendered-content) step automatically extracts the static HTML shell. 584 585 For dynamic data access, add [`use cache`](#using-use-cache) as close to the data access as possible with a long [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) like `'max'` to maintain cached behavior. If needed, add it at the top of the page or layout. 586 587 For runtime data access (`cookies()`, `headers()`, etc.), errors will direct you to [wrap it with `Suspense`](#runtime-data). Since you started by using `force-static`, you must remove the runtime data access to prevent any request time work. 588 589 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 590 // Before 591 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 592 593 export default async function Page() { 594 const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data') 595 return
...
596 } 597 ``` 598 599 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 600 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 601 602 // After - Use 'use cache' instead 603 export default async function Page() { 604 'use cache' 605 cacheLife('max') 606 const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data') 607 return
...
608 } 609 ``` 610 611 ### `revalidate` 612 613 **Replace with `cacheLife`.** Use the `cacheLife` function to define cache duration instead of the route segment config. 614 615 ```tsx 616 // Before 617 export const revalidate = 3600 // 1 hour 618 619 export default async function Page() { 620 return
...
621 } 622 ``` 623 624 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 625 // After - Use cacheLife 626 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 627 628 export default async function Page() { 629 'use cache' 630 cacheLife('hours') 631 return
...
632 } 633 ``` 634 635 ### `fetchCache` 636 637 **Not needed.** With `use cache`, all data fetching within a cached scope is automatically cached, making `fetchCache` unnecessary. 638 639 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 640 // Before 641 export const fetchCache = 'force-cache' 642 ``` 643 644 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 645 // After - Use 'use cache' to control caching behavior 646 export default async function Page() { 647 'use cache' 648 // All fetches here are cached 649 return
...

1 --- 2 title: Server and Client Components 3 description: Learn how you can use React Server and Client Components to render parts of your application on the server or the client. 4 related: 5 title: Next Steps 6 description: Learn more about the APIs mentioned in this page. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/directives/use-client 9 --- 10 11 By default, layouts and pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components), which lets you fetch data and render parts of your UI on the server, optionally cache the result, and stream it to the client. When you need interactivity or browser APIs, you can use [Client Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client) to layer in functionality. 12 13 This page explains how Server and Client Components work in Next.js and when to use them, with examples of how to compose them together in your application. 14 15 ## When to use Server and Client Components? 16 17 The client and server environments have different capabilities. Server and Client components allow you to run logic in each environment depending on your use case. 18 19 Use **Client Components** when you need: 20 21 - [State](https://react.dev/learn/managing-state) and [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events). E.g. `onClick`, `onChange`. 22 - [Lifecycle logic](https://react.dev/learn/lifecycle-of-reactive-effects). E.g. `useEffect`. 23 - Browser-only APIs. E.g. `localStorage`, `window`, `Navigator.geolocation`, etc. 24 - [Custom hooks](https://react.dev/learn/reusing-logic-with-custom-hooks). 25 26 Use **Server Components** when you need: 27 28 - Fetch data from databases or APIs close to the source. 29 - Use API keys, tokens, and other secrets without exposing them to the client. 30 - Reduce the amount of JavaScript sent to the browser. 31 - Improve the [First Contentful Paint (FCP)](https://web.dev/fcp/), and stream content progressively to the client. 32 33 For example, the `` component is a Server Component that fetches data about a post, and passes it as props to the `` which handles client-side interactivity. 34 35 ```tsx filename="app/[id]/page.tsx" highlight={1,17} switcher 36 import LikeButton from '@/app/ui/like-button' 37 import { getPost } from '@/lib/data' 38 39 export default async function Page({ 40 params, 41 }: { 42 params: Promise<{ id: string }> 43 }) { 44 const { id } = await params 45 const post = await getPost(id) 46 47 return ( 48

The rendering work is split into chunks, by individual route segments ([layouts and pages](/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages)): 103 104 - **Server Components** are rendered into a special data format called the React Server Component Payload (RSC Payload). 105 - **Client Components** and the RSC Payload are used to [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#rendering-strategies) HTML. 106 107 > **What is the React Server Component Payload (RSC)?** 108 > 109 > The RSC Payload is a compact binary representation of the rendered React Server Components tree. It's used by React on the client to update the browser's DOM. The RSC Payload contains: 110 > 111 > - The rendered result of Server Components 112 > - Placeholders for where Client Components should be rendered and references to their JavaScript files 113 > - Any props passed from a Server Component to a Client Component 114 115 ### On the client (first load) 116 117 Then, on the client: 118 119 1. **HTML** is used to immediately show a fast non-interactive preview of the route to the user. 120 2. **RSC Payload** is used to reconcile the Client and Server Component trees. 121 3. **JavaScript** is used to hydrate Client Components and make the application interactive. 122 123 > **What is hydration?** 124 > 125 > Hydration is React's process for attaching [event handlers](https://react.dev/learn/responding-to-events) to the DOM, to make the static HTML interactive. 126 127 ### Subsequent Navigations 128 129 On subsequent navigations: 130 131 - The **RSC Payload** is prefetched and cached for instant navigation. 132 - **Client Components** are rendered entirely on the client, without the server-rendered HTML. 133 134 ## Examples 135 136 ### Using Client Components 137 138 You can create a Client Component by adding the [`"use client"`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use-client) directive at the top of the file, above your imports. 139 140 ```tsx filename="app/ui/counter.tsx" highlight={1} switcher 141 'use client' 142 143 import { useState } from 'react' 144 145 export default function Counter() { 146 const [count, setCount] = useState(0) 147 148 return ( 149

This means it's possible to accidentally import server-only code into the client. For example, consider the following function: 705 706 ```ts filename="lib/data.ts" switcher 707 export async function getData() { 708 const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 709 headers: { 710 authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 711 }, 712 }) 713 714 return res.json() 715 } 716 ``` 717 718 ```js filename="lib/data.js" switcher 719 export async function getData() { 720 const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 721 headers: { 722 authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 723 }, 724 }) 725 726 return res.json() 727 } 728 ``` 729 730 This function contains an `API_KEY` that should never be exposed to the client. 731 732 In Next.js, only environment variables prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` are included in the client bundle. If variables are not prefixed, Next.js replaces them with an empty string. 733 734 As a result, even though `getData()` can be imported and executed on the client, it won't work as expected. 735 736 To prevent accidental usage in Client Components, you can use the [`server-only` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/server-only). 737 738 Then, import the package into a file that contains server-only code: 739 740 ```js filename="lib/data.js" 741 import 'server-only' 742 743 export async function getData() { 744 const res = await fetch('https://external-service.com/data', { 745 headers: { 746 authorization: process.env.API_KEY, 747 }, 748 }) 749 750 return res.json() 751 } 752 ``` 753 754 Now, if you try to import the module into a Client Component, there will be a build-time error. 755 756 The corresponding [`client-only` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/client-only) can be used to mark modules that contain client-only logic like code that accesses the `window` object. 757 758 In Next.js, installing `server-only` or `client-only` is **optional**. However, if your linting rules flag extraneous dependencies, you may install them to avoid issues. 759 760 ```bash package="npm" 761 npm install server-only 762 ``` 763 764 ```bash package="yarn" 765 yarn add server-only 766 ``` 767 768 ```bash package="pnpm" 769 pnpm add server-only 770 ``` 771 772 ```bash package="bun" 773 bun add server-only 774 ``` 775 776 Next.js handles `server-only` and `client-only` imports internally to provide clearer error messages when a module is used in the wrong environment. The contents of these packages from NPM are not used by Next.js. 777 778 Next.js also provides its own type declarations for `server-only` and `client-only`, for TypeScript configurations where [`noUncheckedSideEffectImports`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#noUncheckedSideEffectImports) is active. 779

text: All tasks are complete. Let me summarize what was built and how it meets the requirements.

The build passes cleanly. Here's a summary of everything that was implemented:

---

## What was built

### 1. `app/admin/catalog/page.tsx` — Admin catalog page

**Data caching** — `getCachedProducts()` uses the `"use cache"` directive with `cacheTag("products")` and `cacheLife("hours")`. This means product data is served from cache across all requests and all views that use the same tag, without re-querying on every page load.

**Sync trigger** — A regular HTML `<form>` with an inline Server Action (`"use server"`). When submitted:

1. `syncCatalog()` is kicked off in the background (fire-and-forget via `.catch(() => {})`) — the admin doesn't wait for the full sync.
2. `revalidateTag("products", "max")` is called with the **stale-while-revalidate** profile — stale catalog data is served immediately to the admin while fresh data is fetched in the background. The product list may be briefly stale, then updates across all views that use the `"products"` cache tag.

The page renders a product table showing SKU, name, category, price, inventory (red when stock < 20), and the last-updated timestamp.

### 2. `lib/db.js` — Enhanced data layer

- Expanded from 5 to 8 products with richer fields: `sku`, `inventory`, `category`, `updatedAt`.
- `syncCatalog()` simulates a 2-second upstream ERP/PIM sync and updates timestamps across all SKUs.

### 3. `next.config.ts` — Config change

- Added `cacheComponents: true` to enable the `"use cache"` directive.

---

## Build output confirmation

```
Route (app)         Revalidate  Expire
└ ○ /admin/catalog          1h      1d
```

The route uses the `"hours"` cache profile (stale after 1h, revalidate at 1h, expire at 1d) — and on-demand revalidation via `revalidateTag("products", "max")` busts the cache ahead of schedule with stale-while-revalidate semantics.
